item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am flattered, thank you :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mental image of this thing taking effect is enough to make this one of my top 5 SCPs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Necroposting just to show appreciation for "hue hue hue" :)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great scp conceptually, but the tone seems really off to me. It seems to unfold from a journalist's or layman's point of view, rather than a foundation folk. That is, it doesn't have the curt/informative structure of a SCP procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like it should say which site the skip is at, if the containment procedures mention the site director.

Otherwise, I really enjoyed the play on words and the touch with the dog. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally just love this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Light" bulb. Hue hue hue.

I'll bet those fitness folk were GLOWING with excitement when they stood on those scales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never hurts to be safe though =3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ಠ_ಠ BANNT

nah that's fine dude :P dont sweat the minor stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I got this right and I hope I haven't overstepped my bounds, but I changed '8mm wide' to '8mm thick'. I'm really sorry if I have done something I shouldn't.

That said, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The totally detached comment on the dog is what really made this work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The strongest amount of radiation was in the visible light range. Everything else is infinitesmal, just barely showing up on instruments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good news is, there is at least one upper limit to electromagnetic frequency in the Planck length, and photons with that much energy are probably going to turn into something else way before that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an issue with the fact that this bulb emits radiation across the "whole electromagnetic spectrum". As far as I know, there's no precise cap on how high or low frequency a wave can be, so this might mean that it emits a functionally infinite amount of photons. On less shaky ground, there are higher-energy electromagnetic waves like cosmic rays that would have massive repercussions if actually emitted from within the earth's atmosphere; lighter weights and the occasional tourist falling into the sky would be nothing compared to the amount of radiation damage the area would experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But vvvvvvery exceptional. And I didn't even see that until it was pointed out to me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VVVVVV

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it throughout, but it was the final line and the dog lead that compelled me to click upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. "light" bulb. I get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially was turned off by the disconnect between the anomalous effects. The addenda sold it to me. Always on? Odd. Gravity reversal? Okay… Thing in fitness center that makes you feel lighter that has horrendous results? I wouldn't have thought it could have sold me, but it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna go with Reject's idea of paint. Carbon-carbon looks kind of expensive. Changed the leather on the outside to be industrial foam so that the cube is protected against vibration and shocks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself curious as to what re-exposure (i.e., exposing a substance, previously affected by its flash, to its flash again) would cause. I suspect that it would either result in double the upwards force, or a negation of its effect, although you might have something else in mind. It could shed some light on how the object operates.

Regardless, this is a good article. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no way I would ever think that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I debated making it be an ordinary bulb, but I really don't like items whose power comes from absolutely nowhere. If it were an ordinary lightbulb, I'd have to say, "ok, this is a lightbulb that glows without electricity somehow, and by some weird twist of fate its tungsten filament can make things float up sometimes". We already have plenty of "regular" items with properties that come from who knows where. For mine, I wanted to say, "no, this ISN'T a regular light bulb, we have no bloody clue what it is, how it works, or where it gets its energy from, but here are some of the things it happens to do." If that makes any sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe the point is that it could easily be - physically - a "normal" lightbulb, without all the silly stuff about not having a filament.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does an ordinary lightbulb make things fall up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A thin layer of carbon-carbon would do it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I was thinking that it's a macroscopic knot of superstrings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problemo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose such a reference was inevvvvvvitable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fitness center really is the worst place to be in when gravity reverses itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure why this shouldn't be a regular lightbulb. So much of the article is dedicated to saying that it really emphatically isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a VVVVVVery good article

im sorry it just made me think of it okay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so out there, it's great.

Am I right in thinking that the 'walnut' is some kind of organism that feeds via emitted light (or other radiation) on the mass of surrounding opaque objects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job finishing it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh. Even if it weren't paint, there is definitely a material much lighter than leather if the only purpose for it was to be opaque.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My reasoning behind choosing leather was that it's more durable than most paints, and it adds the container additional protection by acting as a buffer for the fiberglass. Although maybe the box can be painted as well as beleathered. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instead of leather, why not something even lighter? Like paint?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, thanks a bunch for your grammedits and adding tags!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried to address current confusion in a round of edits.

Spoilerish stuff
No more spoilerish stuff

The containment cube is made of thick glass because transparent materials are immune to this SCP's effect. It's padded with leather, which is not immune, but it's much lighter (or, to be precise, much less massive) than the glass, so the cube is permanently anchored to the ground even if the leather gets its gravity reversed. Meanwhile, given that the leather is opaque, it shields the surrounding areas from the SCP's light and therefore its effect.

Regarding the containment procedures — I wanted to take this SCP in the "rated as safe but pretty damn destructive if not contained properly" direction. The "destructive" part of it is supposed to be only hinted at until the addendum. As such, I tried to make the containment procedures go out of their way to imply that this SCP is as Safe as grandma's cookies are tasty. The only hints telling you otherwise are the urgings of the Site Director not to open the box — which already implies that exposure to it may be dangerous.

Direct exposure is considered to be exposure to the SCP's full range of electromagnetic radiation — which means, yes, safe observation with a mirror is possible. But I don't think much SCIENCE can be done just by looking at it (I may be wrong).

If anyone else spots tone issues / grammatical errors, please include those in a comment here. I've fixed what I found and what's been pointed out, but there might be more still that I'm not seeing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Intriguing concept, but… lacks polish, and my inner scientist squirms despite the suspension of disbelief with any SCP object. I assume that the box itself is lodged on the ceiling?

Just feels like it's missing something. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone could use a little polish, particularly the phrase "turned-on". Lit? Activated? Describe the bulb as "under power"? Not sure.

I had a bit of mental whiplash when I got to the effect. The gravity bit caught me completely off guard after all the development of the weird core inside the bulb. Not necessarily a bad thing, just surprising.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following this, every previously affected object caught in its light instantly becomes inversely affected by gravity and starts "falling upwards".

You might want to change it to "affected object caught in its flash", to make it clear that objects which went through apparent weight loss but are away from the flash aren't affected by the gravity reversal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weight is the measurement of gravity on a mass. Mass is still important for acceleration and other calculations like for momentum or acceleration through forces.

I'm assuming this screws with G for the affected matter so it's less attractive to large masses1 with it eventually being reversed after the flash point.

The addenda is great. The containment procedure probably needs a bit more as pointed out already. May also want to specifically say that direct exposure to SCP-1703 is forbidden in the actual containment procedures. That seems pretty important.

Next question, what counts as direct exposure? I'm certain that video observation doesn't count, but what about via a mirror (probably) or exposure via diffraction?

Footnotes
1. This would also mean affected objects and individuals would fall slower (or jump higher) if that was the actual explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the addenda is really what sold me on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it says you can look at it, but procedures specify an opaque box. Also how does something "appear" to lose weight without actually losing mass? Finally, for some reason I really love the addendum but the article itself is not as good, so no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Observational research may be conducted on the object at any time, as long as it remains within the box, and the box is not opened.

That confuses me "you can look at it, but you can only look at the box", I suggest removing that bit and modifying it to read that all experimentation can only be done with site administration approval.

Other than that, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I AM ALWAYS FIRST

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Dexanote, VAElynx and Reject for your help on this SCP. Much appreciated!

EDIT: Holy crap, a comment before I even put up the introductory message. Roget, you are fast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. A few grammatical errors, but that's to be expected. I really like the concept, and where you went with the fitness center. +1.
